questions.text: Don't worry, Usman is used to _for long hours because he has been working as a professional driver for 20 years.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: drive - This is the base form of the verb. It is incorrect in this context because 'used to' requires a gerund.
driving - This is the gerund form of the verb and is correct because 'used to' is followed by a gerund to indicate a habitual action.
have driven - This is the present perfect form of the verb. It is incorrect because 'used to' does not pair with this tense.
drove - This is the simple past tense of the verb. It is incorrect in this context as 'used to' requires a gerund, not a past tense.

questions.explanation: driving - This is the gerund form of the verb and is correctbecause "used to" is followed by a gerund.

while 'like' can indicate comparison, it is not grammatically correct in this context as it does not establish the necessary relationship between the ideas presented.

questions.text: Fill in the blank with the appropriate option:He went off the day after I _my last haircut about a month ago.

options.explanation: To get means infinitive, grammatically wrong here.
Went accounts for the simple past, but cannot indicate the earlier action
Got- simple past, less precise than past perfect in formal writing.

questions.explanation: In English, when talking about two events in the past, the earlier one is expressed in past perfect (had + past participle), and the later one in simple past.

questions.text: Fill in the blank with the appropriate option:In this issue of Chips, the dislike _ to a point where it conquered fear and demolished even respect.

options.explanation: "Raised" implies an action of lifting or elevating something, which does not fit the context of the sentence.
"Risen" is the past participle of "rise" and is used with auxiliary verbs, making it grammatically incorrect in this sentence structure.
"Rose" is the simple past tense of "rise," which correctly fits the context of overcoming dislike and fear as described in the sentence.
"Had raised" implies a completed action in the past but is not suitable here as it disrupts the flow of the sentence.

questions.text: Fill in the blank with the appropriate option:To introduce a group of slum boys to the serene environment of better-class youngsters seemed at first _ a wanton stirring of all kinds of things that had better left untouched.

options.explanation: A) Although 'to be' is an infinitive form, it is not the correct choice here as the structure requires a simple form of the verb.

C) 'Been' is the past participle form of 'be' and is not suitable in this context as it does not fit the verb structure required by 'seem.'
D) 'Being' is the gerund form and cannot complete the sentence correctly as it does not align with the linking verb 'seem.'

questions.explanation: The structure " to be” is the correct form when expressing how something appears or seems.

This option is incorrect because 'buy' and 'copy' should be in the gerund form ('buying' and 'copying') to maintain parallel structure in the sentence, which is essential for clarity and grammatical accuracy.

The verb 'think' is used correctly in this context, as it expresses the action of considering. There is no error here.
The preposition 'without' is correctly used to indicate the absence of something. Therefore, it does not contain an error.

options.text: We might have buy a larger house if we had more money.

options.text: We might buy a larger house if we had had more money.

options.text: We might buy larger house if we had more money.

options.text: We might buy a larger house if we had more money.

options.text: My wife is dead and my child is dead, and I wish I was dead myself.

options.text: My wife is dead and my child is dead, and I wish I were dead myself.

options.text: My wife is dead and my child is dead, and I wish I had been dead myself.

options.text: My wife is died and my child is dead, and I wish I were dead myself.

options.text: Her parents live nearby, so she can't help but go there on holidays.

options.text: I think I told you years ago that I had wanted the new style used throughout the School.

options.text: I think I told you years ago that I wanted the new style used throughout the School.

options.text: I think I had told you years ago that I wanted the new style used throughout the School.

questions.subject: English

options.text: After the family had breakfast, they went to the zoo.
options.explanation: This option uses the simple past tense for both actions, suggesting they occurred at the same time. However, it does not correctly indicate that breakfast was completed before going to the zoo.

options.text: After the family had breakfast, they had gone to the zoo.
options.explanation: This option incorrectly uses the past perfect tense for the main action of going to the zoo. Although it correctly shows that breakfast was completed, it suggests that going to the zoo occurred before both events, which is not accurate.

options.text: After the family had had breakfast, they had gone to the zoo.
options.explanation: This option uses the past perfect tense for both actions, which indicates that both events were completed before another event. However, it does not properly indicate the sequence of actions as it makes it ambiguous.

options.text: After the family had had breakfast, they went to the zoo.
options.explanation: This option correctly uses the past perfect tense for breakfast, indicating it was completed before the family went to the zoo, which is correctly stated in the simple past tense.

questions.explanation: “Had had” is the past perfect tense, used to show thatbreakfast was completed before they went to the zoo.“Went” is the simple past for the main action that happenedafter breakfast.
